narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your first-year school decile of 1, third-year decile of 3, undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 29.50, upper-middle family income, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 41% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your first-year academic decile, which placed you at the bottom of your class—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—and signaled early struggles that often correlate with bar challenges. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your LSAT score, which, though below the average of around 37, still nudged the prediction slightly toward passing by showing some standardized testing ability. Your undergraduate GPA also helped in a similar way, being a bit lower than the typical 3.21 but contributing positively compared to even weaker records the model has seen. Overall, your early academic performance outweighed these mitigating factors, setting you apart from peers who start stronger and align more closely with bar passers. While your full-time status matches most students and your family background is solid, they played smaller roles here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male pursuing law—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward or above average, strengthening your early college habits through additional coursework or tutoring, and perhaps gaining practical experience like internships to build a more competitive profile. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to approval next cycle. You're clearly motivated; channeling that into these steps will make a real difference.
